Title: Solution of Proj4. Scheme Interpreter of CS61A (2021-Fall)
URL: https://martinlwx.github.io/en/proj4-scheme-interpreter-of-cs61a-of-ucb-fa21
Content: > Implement the `define` and `lookup` methods of the Frame class…`bindings` is a dictionary representing the bindings in the frame…`parent` is the parent Frame instance…The *environment* for a `Frame` instance consists of that frame, its parent frame, and all its ancestor frames, including the Global Frame. To implement the `define` function, you just need to save the `{ symbol: value }` in `self.bindings`. ...def define(self, symbol, value): def define(self, symbol, value): def define(self, symbol, value): """Define Scheme SYMBOL to have VALUE."""  """Define Scheme SYMBOL to have VALUE.""" """Define Scheme SYMBOL to have VALUE.""" self.bindings[symbol] = value  self.bindings[symbol] = value self. Errors if SYMBOL is not found."""  """Return the value bound to SYMBOL. we check if the symbol is in the current frame  # Case 1. we check if the symbol is in the current frame if symbol in self.bindings.keys():  if symbol in self.bindings.keys(): if symbol in self.

Title: [PDF] CS61A Lecture 34 - Amir Kamil
URL: https://amirkamil.com/teaching/sp13/slides/34-Interpreters_6pp.pdf
Content: '<expression> is shorthand for (quote <expression>) The scheme_read parser converts shorthand to a combination (quote (1 2)) '(1 2) Lambda Expressions Lambda expressions evaluate to user‐defined procedures (lambda (<formal-parameters>) <body>) (lambda (x) (* x x)) class LambdaProcedure(object): def __init__(self, formals, body, env): self.formals = formals self.body = body self.env = env A scheme list of symbols A scheme expression A Frame instance Frames and Environments A frame represents an environment by having a parent frame Frames are Python instances with methods lookup and define In Project 4, Frames do not hold return values g: Global frame y z 3 5 [parent=g] x z 2 4 Define Expressions Define expressions bind a symbol to a value in the first frame of the current environment (define <name> <expression>) (define (<name> <formal parameters>) <body>) (define <name> (lambda (<formal parameters>) <body>)) Procedure definition is a combination of define and lambda Evaluate the <expression> Bind <name> to the result (define method of the current Frame) (define x 2) Applying User‐Defined Procedures Create a new frame in which formal parameters are bound to argument values, whose parent is the env of the procedure Evaluate the body of the procedure in the environment that starts with this new frame (define (f s) (if (null?

"""Apply Scheme PROCEDURE to argument values ARGS (a Scheme list) in. return procedure.apply(args, env). def eval\_all(expressions, env):. """Evaluate each expression im the Scheme list EXPRESSIONS in. environment ENV and return the value of the last.""". return scheme\_eval(expressions.first, env, True). scheme\_eval(expressions.first, env). return eval\_all(expressions.second, env). if self.parent is None:. def define(self, symbol, value):. if symbol in self.bindings:. """Apply SELF to ARGS in ENV, where ARGS is a Scheme list. return self.env.make\_child\_frame(self.formals, args). value = scheme\_eval(expressions.second.first, env).

Title: Interpreters - CS61A Notes
URL: https://cs61a.rouxl.es/docs/week-12/content/interpreters
Content: Our `scheme_eval` function will choose its execution behaviour based on the expression form given to it:. * If there’s a symbol in the expression, try to look up the value in the frame. * All other *legal* expressions are represented as Scheme lists (this could be normal call expressions or special forms) - these are called combinations. If the first token (after the bracket) matches that of a *known* special form, we use a different evaluation procedure, else we just do the normal scheme special from evaluation procedure. Similarly to Python, Scheme variable looks at frames to find the values bound to symbols. When applying user-defined procedures, we need to create a new frame where the arguments of the functions are defined, and then makes it such that the parent of the frame is just the `env` attribute of the procedure (you will see this in the Scheme Project code).

Title: cs61a-projects/scheme/scheme.py at master - GitHub
URL: https://github.com/MattZhao/cs61a-projects/blob/master/scheme/scheme.py
Content: """A Scheme interpreter and its read-eval-print loop.""". def scheme\_eval(expr, env, \_=None): # Optional third argument is ignored. >>> expr = read\_line("(+ 2 2)"). >>> scheme\_eval(expr, create\_global\_frame()). return scheme\_atomp(expr) or scheme\_stringp(expr) or expr is okay. def scheme\_apply(procedure, args, env):. """Apply Scheme PROCEDURE to argument values ARGS in environment ENV.""". return apply\_primitive(procedure, args, env). new\_env = make\_call\_frame(procedure, args, env). def eval\_all(expressions, env):. """Evaluate a Scheme list of EXPRESSIONS & return the value of the last.""". scheme\_eval(expressions[x], env). return scheme\_eval(expressions[len(expressions)-1], env, True). def apply\_primitive(procedure, args\_scheme\_list, env):. """Apply PrimitiveProcedure PROCEDURE to ARGS\_SCHEME\_LIST in ENV. args = list(args\_scheme\_list) # Convert a Scheme list to a Python list. def make\_call\_frame(procedure, args, env):. """Make a frame that binds the formal paramters of PROCEDURE to ARGS.""". new\_f = procedure.env.make\_child\_frame(procedure.formals, args). new\_f = env.make\_child\_frame(procedure.formals, args). in a Scheme list of formal parameters FORMALS are bound to the Scheme. a Scheme list of BODY expressions, and a parent environment that. env.bindings[target] = scheme\_eval(expressions[1], env). if scheme\_true(scheme\_eval(expressions[0], env)):. return scheme\_eval(expressions[1],env, True).
